Evaluation of Emerging Air Sensor Performance
=============================================

**On this page:**

*   [What do the sensor terms used in these tables mean?](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#meaning_of_sensor_terms)
*   [Where can I get information about the air pollutants measured by these sensors?](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#info_on_air_pollutants)
    *   [Particulate matter (PM) sensors](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#pm_sensors)
    *   [Gas Phase sensors](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#gp_sensors)
    *   [CAIRSENSE-Denver Sensor Data Completeness](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#table2_sensor_data)
    *   [Regression and Precision Results for CAIRSENSE sensors (1-hour time averaged)](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#table3_regression)

*   [Related links](https://www.epa.gov/air-sensor-toolbox/evaluation-emerging-air-sensor-performance#related_links)

Emerging air quality sensors – with general traits of being more compact, directly reading pollutants, and lower in cost than traditional methods - have a wide appeal to professional researchers, community groups, students, and citizen scientists alike. Since this technology is still under development, little information exists on the quality of data that these sensors produce.

To help those interested in using sensors as part of air monitoring projects, EPA researchers evaluate sensors for how well they measure air pollutants and how easy they are to use. Placing the sensors near a regulation grade monitor, researchers collect data on air quality with both technologies. By assessing these data collected under the same air quality and weather conditions, researchers can compare how accurate and reliable low-cost technologies are to traditional methods.

The following air sensors have been evaluated in laboratory or field studies by EPA researchers. To assist the user, Sensor Evaluation Tables have been created that provide selected sensor performance results from reports, which can be found on the Air Sensor Toolbox. Note that the results provided in these tables are a subset of all of the sensors and tests conducted, some results are not shown in these summary tables for sensors that had inconclusive results due to either data logging failure, reference monitor failure, or other limiting factors. The full information on all test results are available in the full-length reports. EPA researchers continue to test sensors in various field or laboratory studies, and emerging results will be added to these tables over time.

Any mention of trade names or commercial products does not constitute endorsement. Note that cost information is not reported here, as the market prices of sensors are at the purview of the manufacturer or distributors, and may change with time or purchasing volume.

What do the sensor terms used in these tables mean?
---------------------------------------------------

*   **Detection approach** – Explains how the sensor measures a particular pollutant.
*   **Operating details** – Additional information on the general design of the device, data output rate, and data units
*   **Test results**
    *   **Testing Environment** – Explains how the test was conducted. Field test environments include information on the pollution levels experienced during the test. Laboratory test environments provide the concentration ranges tested.
    *   **Reference monitor used**– Information on the federal reference or equivalent method used as a comparison point against the sensor.
    *   **Averaging (Avg) time; testing period** – Information on how the sensor and reference monitor data were averaged and how long the test was conducted.
    *   **R 2** – R 2 is a statistical term. For these studies, it represents how well a sensor performs in comparison to the reference monitoring technologies. A value of R 2=1 would mean the sensor has perfect correlation to the reference monitor, and R 2=0 would mean no correlation to a reference monitor.

Where can I get information about the air pollutants measured by these sensors?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   [Main AirNow page](https://www.airnow.gov/)
*   [AirNow – Ozone and Your Health](https://www.airnow.gov/sites/default/files/2020-02/ozone-c.pdf)
*   [Main Criteria Air Pollutants page](https://www.epa.gov/criteria-air-pollutants)

| Alphasense OPC N2 | Optical particle counting (0.38 to 17 microns). The cumulative particle counts across various size designations are converted to estimates of PM2.5 and PM10 particle mass concentrations. | Unit was integrated into a prototype United Nations multi-pollutant sensor pod. Designed as a stationary monitor; it recorded data as 1 minute averages in units of µg/m 3. | One month (November 2016) of continuous testing at an EPA testing platform in Research Triangle Park, NC | GRIMM EDM 180 FEM PM2.5 Monitor | 1 h averaging period; Approximately 1 month of continuous data collections | 0.007 (PM2.5) 0.01 (PM10) | [Performance Evaluation of the United Nations Environment Programme Air Quality Monitoring Unit report](https://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=336950&Lab=NERL) |

b Comparison results shown are for the PM 1 channel, which had the highest correlation with the reference monitor. The PM 2.5 channel had significant outliers that were unexplained.

Gas Phase Sensors
-----------------

| Sensor model (pollutant types) | Detection approach | Operating details | Test Results |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Test Environment | Reference monitor used | Avg time; testing period | R 2 | Citation |
| AQMesh (ozone) | All gases are detected by air passing over electrochemical cells. | Measures NO, NO2, CO, CO2, SO2, O3 (all in ppb). | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 49I ozone monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | 0.39 to 0.45 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| CairClip (ozone) | The sensor pulls in a controlled air flow, which passes over a single electrochemical cell that responds to both nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and O3. To isolate one pollutant or the other, a separate measurement is needed in field tests. In laboratory tests, one gas type is introduced to test the sensor response. | The sensor device is miniature and designed for either portable use or stationary use with purchase of accessory peripherals; can record data as fast as 1 second; data units in ppb. | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 49I ozone monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | 0.82 to 0.94 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| Aeroqual SM50 (ozone) | Gas-sensitive semiconductor (GSS). | Measures O3 in ppm. | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 49I ozone monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | 0.91 to 0.97 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| CairClip (nitrogen dioxide) | The sensor pulls in a controlled air flow, which passes over a single electrochemical cell that responds to both nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and O3. To isolate one pollutant or the other, a separate measurement is needed in field tests. In laboratory tests, one gas type is introduced to test the sensor response. | The sensor device is miniature and designed for either portable use or stationary use with purchase of accessory peripherals; can record data as fast as 1 second; data units in ppb. | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 42C nitrogen dioxide monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | 0.42 to 0.76 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| AQMesh (nitrogen dioxide) | All gases are detected by air passing over electrochemical cells. | Measures NO, NO2, CO, CO2, SO2, O3 (all in ppb). | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 42C nitrogen dioxide monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | 0.14 to 0.32 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| Air Quality Egg (nitrogen dioxide) | The sensor device passes air over an internal metal oxide sensor, which provides a resistance change that is converted to an estimated NO2 concentration. | Designed for indoor use or outdoor use with proper weather shielding. Data are reported in units of ppb. | Minimum 30 day testing period of duplicate or triplicate monitors at a state regulatory monitoring site in hot and humid conditions in Decatur, GA. | Thermo Fisher Scientific FEM 42C nitrogen dioxide monitor | hourly comparisons, minimum 30 days | -0.25 to -0.22 | [Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project](http://www.atmos-meas-tech-discuss.net/amt-2016-131/) |
| WT-SU1 Dynamo (ozone) | Ozone (O 3) is detected via a metal oxide semiconductor. | Sensor is incorporated into a weather station; data are output in units of parts per billion (ppb). | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor O 3 ranged 0-400 ppb. | 2B Model 205 O 3 Analyzer | 1 min; several hours | O 3: 0.95 | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| CairClip NO 2/O 3, USB version (ozone) | The sensor pulls in a controlled air flow, which passes over a single electrochemical cell that responds to both nitrogen dioxide (NO 2) and O 3. To isolate one pollutant or the other, a separate measurement is needed in field tests. In laboratory tests, one gas type is introduced to test the sensor response. | The sensor device is miniature and designed for either portable use or stationary use with purchase of accessory peripherals; can record data as fast as 1 second; data units in ppb. | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor O 3 ranged 0-400 ppb; NO 2 ranged 0-200 ppb. | 2B Model 205 O 3 Analyzer; Thermo Model 42C NO/NO 2/NO x Analyzer | 1 min; several hours | O 3: 1.0 NO 2: 1.0 | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| AirCasting (nitrogen dioxide) | The sensor device passes air over an internal metal oxide (MiCS-271) sensor, which provides a resistance change that is converted to an estimated NO 2 concentration. | The sensor device is miniature and designed for portable use, integrated with a smartphone app; can record data as fast as 1 minute; data units in ppb. | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor NO 2 ranged 0-200 ppb. | Thermo Model 42C NO/NO 2/NO x Analyzer | 1 min; several hours | NO 2: 0.98 | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| Platypus (nitrogen dioxide) | Nitrogen dioxide is detected using a thin film liquid crystal mounted to a metal strip. Each measurement of NO 2 requires a new material strip to be entered into the device. | Designed as a handheld monitor; each data point requires a manual filter change, which can be conducted at variable time intervals; sensor output is in units of ppb. | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor NO 2 ranged 0-200 ppb. | Thermo Model 42C NO/NO 2/NO x Analyzer | Several minutes of sample time per test interval (5 concentrations tested) | NO 2: 0.80 | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| CitiSense (nitrogen dioxide a) | All gases are detected by air passing over electrochemical cells (O 3: O 3-3E-1 from CityTechnology; CO: NO 2: NO 2-AQ from Alphasense), which provides a resistance change that is converted to an estimated concentration. | Sensor device is designed for portable use; integrated with a smartphone app; can record data as fast as 6 seconds; data units in ppb. | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor O 3 ranged 0-400 ppb; NO 2 ranged 0-200 ppb. | Thermo Model 42C NO/NO 2/NO x Analyzer | 1 min; several hours | NO 2: 0.98 | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| U-Pod (nitrogen dixode, ozone b) | The U-Pod, at the time of testing, detects NO 2 and O 3 via a metal oxide sensors (NO 2: MiCS-2710, O 3: MiCS-2611 from SGX SensorTech), which provides a voltage output that is converted to concentration units. | Sensor device is designed for stationary use; can record data as fast as 5 seconds; data units in ppb. | Laboratory test in a glass exposure chamber with a gas standard introduced at controlled concentrations; Reference monitor O 3 ranged 0-400 ppb; NO 2 ranged 0-200 ppb. | 2B Model 205 O 3 Analyzer; Thermo Model 42C NO/NO 2/NO x Analyzer | 1 min; several hours | O 3: 0.88 NO 2: n/a | [Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=277270&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=sensor+evaluation+report) |
| Unitec SENS-IT (benzene) | The SENS-IT passes air flow over a thick film metal oxide semiconductor, which generates a signal that is converted to a specific gas concentration. | Sensor is miniature, designed for incorporation into stationary or mobile monitoring applications; data at the time of testing were output in voltage units. | Laboratory test in a stainless steel test chamber with specific gases (benzene, 1,3 butadiene, tetrachloroethylene) introduced individually or in mixtures; concentration ranged 0-25 ppb. | Gas chromatography | ~3 hours; 18 hours | Benzene-only: 0.90 | [Next Generation Air Monitoring (NGAM) VOC Sensor Evaluation Report](http://cfpub.epa.gov/si/si_public_record_report.cfm?dirEntryId=308114&simpleSearch=1&searchAll=Next+generation+air+monitoring) |

a The CitiSense monitor also includes sensors for carbon monoxide and ozone, which were not evaluated in the laboratory test.

b The U-Pod also includes sensors for nitric oxide, carbon monoxide, volatile organic compounds, carbon dioxide, and sulfur dioxide, which were not evaluated in this laboratory test.

CAIRSENSE-Denver Study
----------------------

The Community Air Sensor Network (CAIRSENSE) project was a multi-year research project that focused on evaluating performance characteristics and limitations of low-costs sensors. For the second part of the CAIRSENSE study,Denver, Colorado,was chosen to test the sensors’ performance under conditions of high altitude,dry and lower temperature conditions. Beyond assessing sensor performance through correlation with a reference monitor, this study also investigated the degree to which data from sensors is able to produce similar temporal, wind-direction, and transient event trends in comparison to a high time-resolution reference monitor. Citation:Atmos. Meas. Tech. Discuss.,[https://doi.org/10.5194/amt-11-4605-2018](https://doi.org/10.5194/amt-11-4605-2018)

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Aeroqual | 82% | 0% | 18% |
| 73% | 0% | 27% |
| 81% | 5% | 13% |
| Air Assure | 87% | 0% | 13% |
| 87% | 0% | 13% |
| 87% | 0% | 13% |
| AirBeam | 74% | 0% | 25% |
| 62% | 6% | 32% |
| 62% | 6% | 32% |

Advances in environmental pollutant detection techniques: Enhancing public health monitoring and risk assessment
================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Yang Li b d e f g h 1, Biqing Chen c 1, Shuaifei Yang a, Zhe Jiao a, Meichen Zhang a, Yanmei Yang a, Yanhui Gao a

Highlights
----------

*   •Emerging technologies offer rapid and sensitive detection of diverse environmental pollutants. 
*   •Environmental monitoring networks provide real − time data for public health initiatives. 
*   •Challenges like data integration hinder cost − effective detection solution development. 
*   •Interdisciplinary approaches are crucial for comprehensive disease prevention via pollutant detection. 

Abstract
--------

Accurate detection and monitoring of environmental pollutants are of paramount importance for disease prevention and public health. In recent years, the ever-expanding human activities and industrial production have given rise to a sharp increase in the complexity and variety of these pollutants, which pose significant threats to human well − being. Environmental pollutants stem from multiple sources, such as heavy metals, persistent organic pollutants, inorganic non − metallic pollutants, emerging pollutants, and biological contaminants. Traditional detection technologies, though valuable for their sensitivity and accuracy, are constrained by complex sample preparation, poor selectivity, and the absence of standardized detection methods. On the other hand, emerging technologies, including nanotechnology, molecular detection methods, biosensors, Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS), multi-omics, and big data analysis, offer promising solutions for rapid and sensitive pollutant detection. The establishment of environmental monitoring networks and data − sharing platforms further enhances real − time pollutant monitoring and provides solid data support for public health initiatives. Nonetheless, challenges persist, including data integration, exposure assessment, and the development of cost-effective and portable detection solutions. Future progress in interdisciplinary approaches and technology integration will be crucial for advancing environmental pollutant detection and facilitating comprehensive disease prevention. This review systematically classifies environmental pollutants and showcases the latest advancements in detection technologies, offering critical insights for environmental monitoring and public health protection.

------------------

This paper highlights the latest development of detection technology, which provides important insights for environmental monitoring and public health protection.

1. Introduction
---------------

In recent years, the rapid expansion of human activities and industrial production has led to an increasingly complex array of environmental pollutants, significantly impacting human health. The incidence and distribution of pollutant-related diseases are often intricately tied to local environmental conditions and human activities, such as soil composition, water sources, climate, ecosystems, lifestyle, and dietary habits ([Sun, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0005)). These factors contribute to the emergence of various diseases in specific regions or populations, many of which have long-term epidemic characteristics. These diseases manifest in diverse ways, including toxic disorders, nutritional deficiencies, parasitic infections, and infectious diseases. The high prevalence and prolonged duration of these diseases not only degrade the health and quality of life of affected populations but also hinder regional economic growth, social development, and environmental sustainability. The etiology of these diseases is multifaceted, rooted in environmental exposure to pollutants resulting from both natural and anthropogenic activities. Factors such as geological conditions, climate variability, mining, industrial production, agricultural practices, and lifestyle choices play critical roles in influencing the distribution and intensity of environmental pollutants. Furthermore, socioeconomic factors, including infrastructure development, healthcare accessibility, and public hygiene, are equally important in disease transmission and prevention ([Bolan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0010), [Sim et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0015), [Zhang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0020)). Despite the global efforts by governments and international organizations to implement control measures, these diseases continue to persist worldwide. Accurate detection and continuous monitoring of environmental pollutants are essential for designing effective prevention and control strategies. The development of environmental monitoring networks, in combination with advanced detection technologies, provides essential data for identifying pollutant sources, assessing exposure risks, and mitigating the associated health impacts([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#f0005)).

Environmental pollutants arise from both natural and anthropogenic sources, with significant implications for human health. Natural sources of pollution include geological environments, where minerals present in the Earth's crust enter the soil and water through weathering or erosion. For example, high fluoride concentrations in groundwater can lead to excessive fluoride levels in drinking water, contributing to endemic fluorosis ([Misra and Mishra, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0025)). Similarly, high levels of naturally occurring arsenic in groundwater can result in chronic arsenic poisoning ([Shaji et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0030)). Volcanic activity also plays a role, releasing substantial amounts of sulfur dioxide, hydrogen chloride, and trace elements like arsenic, lead, and mercury into the atmosphere, which eventually settle into soil and water bodies, becoming critical contributors to disease. Geographic and climatic factors further exacerbate natural pollution; for instance, prolonged droughts can concentrate pollutants in soils and water bodies, heightening exposure risks ([Yang et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0035)).

On the anthropogenic side, industrial production and mining activities represent major sources of pollution ([Li et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0040)). Emissions from heavy industries, chemical plants, and smelting operations often contain hazardous pollutants such as heavy metals and persistent organic pollutants, which severely contaminate the environment ([Lombard et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0045)). Agricultural practices also contribute to pollution, as the prolonged use of fertilizers and pesticides leads to the accumulation of heavy metals and organic pollutants in soils and water bodies ([Kubicz et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0050)). Certain pesticides containing arsenic and lead can contaminate farmland and water sources, resulting in chronic exposure and poisoning ([Wurtsbaugh et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0055)). Additionally, lifestyle changes introduce new pollution sources, including pharmaceutical residues and personal care products that enter the environment through wastewater, forming emerging pollutants that resist traditional wastewater treatment processes and pose significant health risks ([Rigby et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0060)).

The relationship between environmental pollutants and disease development is increasingly well-documented, with pollutants entering the human body through various pathways such as drinking water, food, and air. These pollutants accumulate at high exposure levels and lead to a wide range of diseases, the characteristics of which vary based on the geographic region, environmental conditions, and levels of exposure. Understanding the mechanisms by which pollutants contribute to disease is crucial for developing effective strategies to mitigate their impact on public health.

As global environmental challenges intensify, the need for effective monitoring and control of pollutants that contribute to disease has become a critical public health concern. Accurate identification and continuous monitoring of these pollutants are paramount for managing the risks they pose and for preventing environment-related diseases. Traditional detection technologies, such as chemical analysis and biological monitoring, remain valuable tools due to their established sensitivity and accuracy. However, these methods often require complex sample processing and lack the efficiency needed for practical applications. Additionally, the coexistence of multiple pollutants in the environment complicates the detection process, as traditional methods typically focus on individual pollutants rather than on their combined effects.

Emerging detection technologies, such as nanotechnology, biosensors, Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS), molecular detection, and multi-omics approaches, have introduced new possibilities for rapid and accurate detection of environmental pollutants. These technologies are not only enhancing sensitivity and selectivity but are also enabling the simultaneous detection of multiple pollutants. The integration of big data analysis and Artificial Intelligence (AI) into pollutant monitoring systems has further revolutionized the field by improving data processing capabilities, enabling more comprehensive assessments of pollutant exposure and its health impacts.

Moreover, the establishment of environmental monitoring networks and information-sharing platforms has provided critical real-time data for managing pollutants. These networks, often enhanced by the Internet of Things (IoT) and sensor technologies, allow for continuous monitoring of air, water, and soil, offering invaluable data to both researchers and policymakers. The combination of these advanced technologies has the potential to significantly improve our understanding of the interactions between pollutants and disease mechanisms, thus enhancing public health protection strategies.

Despite these advancements, emerging detection technologies face several challenges, including the need for further improvements in sample processing, data integration, and the development of cost-effective, portable detection devices. There is also a need for standardized detection protocols and methodologies to ensure consistency and reliability across studies and monitoring systems. As the field continues to evolve, interdisciplinary collaboration will be crucial for overcoming these obstacles and for advancing the detection and control of environmental pollutants.

In the context of growing environmental pollution from human and industrial activities, accurate pollutant detection is vital for public health. This review's novelty lies in its systematic categorization of diverse environmental pollutants, including heavy metals and biological contaminants. It details both the limitations of traditional detection technologies and emerging methods like nanotechnology and SERS ([Karthik et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0065), [Saravanan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0070)). Moreover, it emphasizes environmental monitoring networks and data − sharing platforms, along with challenges such as data integration ([Saravanan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0075)). Pointing towards interdisciplinary and technology − integrated future approaches, it offers unique insights for environmental monitoring and public health protection.

### 1.1. Sources of environmental pollutants and disease impacts

Environmental pollutants are significant contributors to human disease, particularly in regions where biogeochemical processes are disrupted. Identifying the sources of these pollutants is essential for developing effective monitoring and control strategies. One of the primary natural sources of pollution is the geological environment, where minerals from the Earth’s crust infiltrate water and soil systems through natural processes like erosion and weathering.

In addition to natural sources, human activities are accelerating the introduction of pollutants into the environment. Industrial production, mining, and agricultural practices have become significant drivers of environmental contamination. Heavy metals and persistent organic pollutants (POPs), often byproducts of industrial and mining processes, are among the most hazardous contaminants, accumulating in ecosystems and posing long-term health risks. Pesticides and fertilizers used in agriculture contribute to the buildup of toxic substances in soil and water bodies. Moreover, the expansion of urban lifestyles has introduced new classes of pollutants, such as pharmaceuticals and personal care products, into the environment. These contaminants enter water systems via wastewater and resist traditional water treatment processes, ultimately infiltrating the food chain and posing risks to human health ([Zhou et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0080)).

The connection between environmental pollutants and diseases has become increasingly evident, with pollutants entering the human body through various pathways, including ingestion, inhalation, and dermal absorption. These pollutants accumulate over time, causing significant health effects, including cancer, neurological disorders, respiratory diseases, and cardiovascular conditions. Different pollutants exhibit varying levels of toxicity, with specific disease manifestations depending on geographic regions, pollutant types, and levels of exposure. Understanding these connections is crucial for the development of targeted interventions aimed at reducing the health impacts of environmental pollutants([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#f0005)).

### 1.2. The link between environmental pollutants and diseases

The detrimental impact of environmental pollutants on human health has been extensively documented, and the connections between pollutants and diseases are increasingly clear. Pollutants enter the human body through various exposure routes, such as drinking water, food, and air, and can accumulate at high concentrations, resulting in a wide spectrum of health conditions. The manifestation of these diseases varies depending on pollutant types, exposure levels, and geographic regions. The emergence of new pollutants, including pharmaceutical residues, personal care products, microplastics, and nanomaterials, further complicates the landscape of environmental pollution. While the full extent of their health impacts remains under investigation, preliminary studies suggest potential risks associated with long-term exposure to these emerging pollutants. Given the diverse range of pollutants and their varying routes of exposure, it is crucial to develop a comprehensive understanding of how environmental pollutants contribute to disease mechanisms. This knowledge is essential for devising effective strategies for preventing and mitigating the health impacts of environmental pollution. The increasing complexity of pollution highlights the need for innovative detection methods and integrated monitoring systems to better assess and manage these risks.

### 1.3. Major types of biogeochemical disease

Biogeochemical diseases arise from the uneven distribution of chemical elements within the Earth’s crust, leading to either an excess or deficiency of essential elements in local water and soil. These imbalances contribute to specific disease patterns in affected regions. Below are several notable examples of biogeochemical diseases and their associations with environmental pollutants:

**Endemic Fluorosis:**

Fluorosis is a chronic condition resulting from prolonged exposure to high levels of fluoride, primarily affecting teeth and bones ([Reddy et al., 1969](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0085)). The most common forms are dental fluorosis, characterized by mottled tooth enamel, and skeletal fluorosis, which leads to bone deformities and joint stiffness. High fluoride levels in drinking water are the primary cause, particularly in regions such as northwest and northeast China, where groundwater fluoride concentrations are elevated ([Ali et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0090)). In areas where residents burn high-fluoride coal for cooking and heating, such as Guizhou and Sichuan, indoor air pollution exacerbates the problem, causing coal-burning fluorosis through contaminated food and air ([Cao et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0095), [Guo et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0100)).

**Endemic Arsenicosis:**

Chronic arsenic poisoning, or arsenicosis, is caused by exposure to high levels of arsenic, either through drinking water or food ([Astolfi, 1971](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0105), [Carballo, 1987](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0110)). This condition manifests as skin pigmentation changes, keratosis, and damage to the liver, cardiovascular system, and nervous system ([Saha et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0115)). Regions in South America, Bangladesh, India, and parts of China are particularly affected, often due to geological factors that lead to the contamination of groundwater ([Roychowdhury, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0120), [Pokhrel et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0125), [Shahid et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0130)). In some parts of China, burning arsenic-rich coal contributes to arsenicosis by contaminating indoor air and food supplies ([Zhou, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0135)).

**Endemic Goiter and Cretinism:**

These thyroid disorders are caused by iodine deficiency, which leads to the development of goiter (enlarged thyroid gland) and, in severe cases, cretinism, which results in profound intellectual and physical developmental impairments ([Mullinger et al., 1952](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0140)). Iodine-deficient regions, particularly in mountainous areas with poor soil iodine content, are most affected ([Chen and Hetzel, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0145), [Thilly et al., 1977](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0150), [Dunn, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0155)). Additionally, high levels of fluoride, sulfur, or other thyroid-disrupting substances in drinking water may contribute to the prevalence of goiter ([Keith, 1924](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412025001163#b0160), [Burgi et al., 1984](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/art

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: [PDF] Air Quality Sensors | GAO ---
URL: https://www.gao.gov/assets/870/867275.pdf

SUMMARY:
This report assesses lower-cost air quality sensors, their benefits, challenges, and seven policy options to address implementation issues.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Emerging air quality sensors are compact, affordable, and user-friendly, enabling real-time pollutant monitoring. The EPA evaluates these sensors for accuracy and usability in environmental projects.
Mobile applications enhance accessibility to air quality data, allowing users to monitor pollution levels in real-time and make informed decisions about their health and environment.

Collaborative platforms facilitate data sharing among researchers, community groups, and citizens, promoting collective action against pollution and improving public awareness of environmental issues.
